Query: A previously healthy 19-year-old man comes to the physician with right-sided, dull, throbbing scrotal pain for 12 hours. He has also had a burning sensation on urination and increased urinary frequency for the past 3 days. He has not had urethral discharge. He is sexually active with one female partner and does not use condoms. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows a tender right testicle; lifting it provides relief. The penis appears normal, with no discharge at the meatus. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count; urinalysis shows 3 WBC/hpf. A Gram stain of a urethral swab shows polymorphonuclear leukocytes but no organisms. Testicular ultrasound shows increased blood flow to the right testicle compared to the left. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

Options:
A: Neisseria gonorrhoeae infection
B: Testicular tumor
C: Chlamydia trachomatis infection
D: Varicocele

Therefore, it is optimal if the patient refrains from voiding for at least 2 hours before the examination. The examiner should collect both a first voided sample and a mid-stream urine specimen. A bilateral testicular examination should be conducted to identify signs of inflammation or infection. The examiner should check for palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy. A digital rectal examination of the prostate should be performed if necessary. If needed, a specimen of the urethral discharge can be obtained by gently "milking" the penis. This involves compressing the ventral surface of the urethra with a gloved finger, starting at the base of the penis and moving slowly toward the glans. Document [2] (Title: Epididymitis -- Evaluation) Evaluation of the male patient with scrotal pain should begin with urinalysis. Though nonspecific, the presence of red blood cells and white blood cells in the urine may indicate an acute infectious or inflammatory condition. A urethral swab is indicated in cases where the sexually transmitted disease is considered likely given the patient's sexual history. The radiographic evaluation includes ultrasonography with attention not only to the anatomic structure but also to assess vascular flow to the testis. Ultrasonography can demonstrate inflammation of the epididymis and testis in cases of epididymitis and epididymo-orchitis. Diagnosis is based on positive discharge on an exam or more than 2 WBC seen on microscopy or presence of leukocytes on urinalysis  (or 10 WBC on first-void urine catch).
Document [4] (Title: Chlamydia -- History and Physical) Urethritis: It is most commonly seen in men. There are subtle clinical differences between gonococcal urethritis and chlamydial urethritis, but making a reliable distinction without testing is not possible. It presents with dysuria and urethral discharge, which is typically white, gray, or sometimes clear, and may only be evident after penile "stripping" or during morning hours. Women with urethritis may complain of frequency or dysuria and may mistake their symptoms for a urinary tract infection. Urinalysis will reveal pyuria, but neither bacterial culture nor gram stain will reveal organisms.

Document [6] (Title: Dysuria -- Evaluation) Gram stain microscopy showing Gram-negative diplococci is diagnostic for gonorrhea. Document [7] (Title: Sterile Pyuria -- Introduction) Sterile pyuria is a medical condition characterized by the persistence of leukocytes in urine without bacteriuria. Pyuria is characterized by a urine sample containing greater than or equal to 10 white blood cells (WBCs) per mm³, unspun urine with more than 3 WBC per high power field (WBC/HPF), or a urine dipstick analysis showing leukocyte esterase or nitrites. [1] Sterile pyuria occurs when any of these criteria are met without significant bacteriuria on urine culture, defined as greater than or equal to 10 5 colony-forming units (CFUs) per mL.

Prostatitis: Symptoms include dysuria, urinary dysfunction, pelvic pain, and pain with ejaculation. Expressed secretions from the prostate may show increased leukocytes on microscopy. Tenderness upon palpation of the testis itself may indicate the possibility of epididymo-orchitis or orchitis. The skin overlying the scrotum may appear warm, erythematous, inflamed, and indurated as a result of infection. Tender inguinal adenopathy may be present as well. Diagnosis should occur within a clinical context where a urogenital tract infection is the most likely cause. In such cases, empiric treatment with first-line antibiotics is appropriate. A urine sample should also be sent for microscopy and culture before beginning treatment, although that is not always possible. The urine sample almost always shows an abnormal red or white cell count, positive nitrites, and bacteria.

Query: A previously healthy 19-year-old man comes to the physician with right-sided, dull, throbbing scrotal pain for 12 hours. He has also had a burning sensation on urination and increased urinary frequency for the past 3 days. He has not had urethral discharge. He is sexually active with one female partner and does not use condoms. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows a tender right testicle; lifting it provides relief. The penis appears normal, with no discharge at the meatus. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count; urinalysis shows 3 WBC/hpf. A Gram stain of a urethral swab shows polymorphonuclear leukocytes but no organisms. Testicular ultrasound shows increased blood flow to the right testicle compared to the left. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

Options:
A: Neisseria gonorrhoeae infection
B: Testicular tumor

D: Varicocele

Preventive and early‑detection measures are emphasized through التحصّي (screening) for other male genital disorders. This integrated framework organizes genitourinary cancers by organ, anatomical site, and malignant‑tumor grouping, and links them to diagnostic coding, surgical management, and screening strategies essential for comprehensive patient care.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1410) Noninflammatory disorder of the female genital organs refers to a group of gynecologic conditions that are not caused by infection or inflammation. Within this category, two notable manifestations are كيسة رحمية (uterine cyst) and نزيف مهبلي (vaginal bleeding). Both entities are considered clinical outcomes or presentations that can arise from noninflammatory pathology of the female reproductive system, and recognizing them as part of the same disorder spectrum aids clinicians in diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected patients.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1836) The urethra (uretra) serves as the anatomical site where specific clinical findings can be observed. In particular, the finding site of the urethra includes تدلّي الإحليل, which denotes urethral discharge, and انسداد الإحليل, which denotes urethral obstruction. Recognizing these findings at the urethral site is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management of urethral disorders.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1894) The genito urinary system and sex hormones represent a broad medical domain that includes urologicals, indicating that urological topics are considered a subset of the overall system involving the urinary tract and reproductive hormonal functions.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811) Other complications of pregnancy are clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل – اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and also have a relationship with اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1087) The prostate (prostata) is a key anatomical site within the male genital system and is the focus of specific diagnostic procedures that are applied when the prostate tissue has been partially excised. This organ serves as the anatomic location for metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي), linking the disease directly to the prostate tissue. In addition, the prostate is identified as the location of a broader category of prostatic disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي), which functions as a parent classification for other male genital disorders. These other male genital disorders are themselves situated in the prostate and are closely associated with screening activities (التحصّي). Together, these relationships highlight the central role of the prostate in both disease manifestation and diagnostic evaluation, emphasizing the importance of targeted procedures and screening in the management of prostate‑related conditions.
